text: The laparoscopic view reveals fibrinous exudates and fluid collection throughout all quadrants of the abdomen, indicative of a ruptured appendix.
The surgeon carefully performs suction irrigation within the abdominal cavity, removing fluid and fibrinous exudates associated with the ruptured appendix.
The surgeon performs a thorough lavage of the peritoneal cavity during the laparoscopic appendectomy, effectively irrigating the area to manage the perforated appendicitis.
The surgeon encounters difficulty as the omentum attempts to seal the ruptured appendix, requiring careful manipulation to access the appendix for removal.
The surgeon carefully separates adhesions within the abdominal cavity, a necessary step due to the presence of peritonitis and fibrinous exudates from a ruptured appendix.
The omentum is visibly sealing off the appendix, covering the inflamed area with its fatty tissue.
The surgeon carefully separates the omentum from the appendix, using gentle traction to detach the adhesions.
In the pelvic cavity of a male patient, the surgeon uses suction to remove excess fluid. The bladder and appendix are visible, and a Striker mini alligator instrument is used for manipulation.
The surgeon gently lifts the appendix, which is ruptured at the base, while continuing to separate the surrounding omentum from the appendix.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to touch the left side of the appendix, revealing a perforation at its base. The cecum is visible on the right side of the patient, adjacent to the perforation site.
The surgeon highlights the perforation at the base of the appendix, which is visibly amputating out from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes a small stump of the appendix still attached to the cecum, indicating the need for further dissection to detach it completely.
The surgeon uses a Mishra's knot technique to secure the perforated appendix. A striker mini alligator instrument is passed through the loop to assist in the knot application.

The surgeon uses a knot pusher to gently catch the appendix without pulling it out, ensuring it does not amputate in the loop. The knot is secured with a series of hitches and winds, with the tip of the knot pusher positioned over the cecum.
The surgeon carefully tightens the knot around the perforated part of the appendix, ensuring it is positioned at the cecum without applying excessive force.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to amputate the appendix, applying heat to the mucosa to ensure it is burned and disinfected, minimizing the risk of mucosal infection due to the appendix's infected state.
The surgeon removes the appendix, which has been detached from the cecum.
The surgical field is thoroughly irrigated, followed by suction to clear any remaining fluids and debris.

The surgeon performs a vigorous lavage in the surgical area using approximately three liters of saline, ensuring thorough cleaning of the site. An air bubble test is conducted to check for leaks, with saline used to identify any air bubbles indicating leakage.
The surgeon places a drain in the surgical site and uses an endo bag to retrieve the removed appendix, ensuring containment of the specimen.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation while the striker mini alligator holds the appendix, which will be removed later in an endo bag. The knot applied at the cecum is visible, along with the tinea coli, and there is no leak in this area.
The surgeon visually identifies the gallbladder and the appendix within the surgical field.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation in all quadrants of the abdomen, ensuring thorough lavage to prevent fluid accumulation in the diaphragmatic area, which could lead to a subdiaphragmatic abscess.
The surgeon performs repeated suction irrigation across all quadrants of the abdomen, removing fibrinous exudate to maximize cleaning and aspiration.
The surgeon completes peritoneal toileting and introduces a glove as an endo bag into the abdominal cavity for the removal of the appendix.

The surgeon places the appendix into the glove endo bag within the abdominal cavity. The edges of the glove are grasped with an instrument, and the endo bag is pulled into the cannula. The entire cannula is then extracted from the abdominal cavity, necessitating port closure.
After thorough suction and irrigation, a nasogastric tube is introduced as a drain into the abdominal cavity through a ten millimeter extra port. The port is then closed securely.
